entries.end_notes: In translations from BH, the special functionality of w_ before verbal clauses beginning with the verb (consecutive, paratactic, subordinating) and verbal clauses beginning with a nominal (circumstantial) are due to translation language. Consecutive narrative with a preterite is natively attested only in Old Aramaic, while the explicit circumstantial clause functionality seems to have weakened after Middle Aramaic, though it is still quite visible in the earlier Syriac material.

entries.end_notes: The identification with Akkadian amurdinnu, murdinnu is certain. Note especially that the alternate forms in both languages are the same. However the identification as "bramble" rather than "rose" or some other wild-growing thorny bush suggested by CAD A/2 and followed by DJBA is conjectural. The Arabic gloss as "boxthorn" cited by DJBA makes fine sense given the medicinal uses attested for the Akkadian.

citations.translation: it grazes among the boxthorns

entries.end_notes: For bibliog. on the Persian and other parallels to this culture word see Ciancaglini, ILS, 168.
Scientific name: Meropidae.

wrwdq as the name of a demon; see note. That the JBA bowl JBAmb2 99 spells this as ורודק four times, an otherwise unknown word, almost certainly demonstrates that the spell in question was copied from a Syriac original, for only in Syriac can qof and waw easily be confused. Not surprisingly, the names of the clients also are Syriac Christian. Note, too, the strong connection of this word with visions in line 8 of the text.
